title: Blind Quantum Computation on a Modular Superconducting Processor
authors: Yongxin Song, Johannes Knörzer, Kieran Dalton, Andreas Wallraff, Jean-Claude Besse
abstract: Current cloud-based quantum processors offer access to advanced hardware hosted on a remote server, but do not guarantee data or algorithm privacy. Blind quantum computation provides information-theoretic privacy by enabling a client to execute an algorithm without disclosing information about either the task or the final result. Here, we execute a measurement-based blind quantum computation protocol on a superconducting processor comprising two flip-chip-bonded modules, one acting as a server and the other as a client. The server generates a two-dimensional cluster state and forwards it to the client. Using this resource, the client implements a universal gate set with only adaptive single-qubit rotations and measurements. To illustrate this approach, we execute a three-qubit instance of the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm. We analyze the server's quantum state after each rotation of a measurement-based single-qubit gate to verify that negligible information about the computation is revealed to the server, consistent with the one-way flow of information that guarantees blindness. This proof-of-principle demonstration establishes key elements of blind quantum computation in superconducting-circuit architectures, indicating that intermediate-scale implementations of blind protocols may become feasible with realistic near-term improvements in gate fidelities.
summary: This paper demonstrates the first implementation of a blind quantum computation protocol on a modular superconducting processor. By using a server-client architecture, the researchers showed that a client can run algorithms like Deutsch-Jozsa without revealing the task or results to the server. The study quantifies extremely low information leakage, proving the feasibility of secure, privacy-preserving cloud quantum computing.
main_problem: The lack of privacy for algorithms and data in cloud-based quantum computing. The paper aims to demonstrate a proof-of-principle for Blind Quantum Computation (BQC) on a modular superconducting processor.
main_result: Successful execution of a measurement-based BQC protocol and the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm on a modular architecture, with verified negligible information leakage (Holevo information of 2.3e-4 bits) to the server.
method: A measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) protocol where a server prepares a cluster state and a client performs adaptive single-qubit rotations and measurements, using iterative state transfer between modules.
model_or_system: A modular superconducting processor consisting of two flip-chip-bonded modules (a server and a client), each hosting three flux-tunable transmon qubits connected via static qubit-qubit couplers.
key_observables: Cluster state fidelity, process fidelity of T-gates and two-qubit gates, Holevo information for leakage analysis, and Pauli expectation values.
important_parameters: Median T1 of 43 us, median T2* of 39 us, single-qubit gate error of 0.09%, and two-qubit gate error of 0.8%.
main_assumptions: Assumes no information leakage via unintended hardware side channels like crosstalk and assumes the absence of unintended pathways between modules.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental principle and circuit diagrams; Figure 2 shows cluster state fidelity estimation; Figure 3 details measurement-based gate implementation; Figure 4 shows the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm execution; Figure 5 verifies blindness via Pauli operators; Figure 6 illustrates single-qubit rotation patterns; Figure 7 provides qubit coherence and error rate distributions; Figure 8 shows the experimental setup schematic.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the privacy problem in cloud computing, describes the modular hardware architecture, details the MBQC/BQC protocol implementation, presents results for cluster state generation and gate fidelities, demonstrates the Deutsch-Jozsa algorithm, quantifies information leakage, and concludes with an error decomposition and scalability analysis.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a practical implementation of a quantum communication/computing protocol (BQC) using a modular hardware architecture, which is highly relevant to the development of quantum networks and distributed quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Blind quantum computation with a modular superconducting quantum device. (a) Principle of the experiment. The experimental device consists of a server (blue) and a client (red) quantum module, each hosting three transmon qubits (circles) having dedicated control (not shown in the illustration) and readout (yellow) circuits. The server generates the resource cluster state and transfers it to the client. In a feedforward experiment, the readout signal is acquired and processed by the instrument in real-time which generates the conditional drive pulses. Information about the target computation sequence is only acquired by the classical instruments on the client side and not revealed to...

FIG. 2. Fidelity estimation of the resource cluster state. (a) Experimental sequence. Left: Randomly generat- ing a stabilizer. Each circle represent a qubit in the cluster state. The stabilizer is expressed as the product of single- qubit X (red), Y (blue), Z (green), and identity (gray) oper- ators acting on each qubit. Right: Measurement of the sta- bilizer by updating the measurement bases of the mid-circuit and final measurement steps. The generation and measure- ment is repeated Np times for estimating the fidelity of the cluster state. (b) Estimated fidelities of cluster states with width w, depth d, and size n = w × d, see inset for the il- lustration of a generic cluster state....

FIG. 3. Deterministic implementation of measurement-based single- and two-qubit gates. (a) Measurement pattern used to realize arbitrary single- qubit unitaries. The cluster state nodes (gray disks) are measured in the sequence indicated by the arrow, resulting in the output state (black). (b) Experimental process matrix |εin,out| of a deterministic measurement-based T gate (filled bars), realized by setting α1 = −π/4, α2 = α3 = −π/2 in (a). The ideal and simulated processes are shown with solid and dashed wire frames. (c) Measurement-based generation of the output state |ψ⟩= (|00⟩−i|11⟩)/ √

FIG. 5. Verifying the blindness of the protocol. Mea- sured Pauli operator expectations values of (a) the server and (c) the client qubit at different stages of a measurement-based T-gate execution, gate sequence in (b). Solid wire frames show the ideal expectation values. The purity γ of each state is quoted below each tomogram. Each of the first three blocks represents generating and measuring one node along the 1D cluster state in the basis B(αi), i = 1, 2, 3. The last block rep- resents the SWAP circuit between the server and the client module.

FIG. 6. Principle of measurement-based single- qubit rotation. (a) An elementary circuit that realizes a measurement-based single-qubit rotation parameterized by the projection angle θ. Passing an input qubit in state |ψin⟩ and an auxiliary qubit in state |0⟩through the sequence gener- ates the output state |ψout⟩accompanied by the binary mea- surement result s. (b) Three measurement-based single-qubit rotation cells are concatenated to realize arbitrary single- qubit unitary parameterized by the Euler angles (α1, α2, α3).
